title: Economy vs Markets
text: A recurring question: how tightly is the stock market coupled to economic growth? This page puts the two side-by-side, looks at correlations, and lets you drill into S&P 500 sector performance by year.

title: Economy vs Markets
section: Annual GDP growth vs S&P 500 return
text:
Annual GDP growth vs S&P 500 return
Two very different pictures in the same window — equity returns are far more volatile than GDP.

title: Economy vs Markets
text:
How correlated are they, really?
Each point is a year. Hover to see the year label. The 2020 COVID year and the 2022 inflation shock pull the relationship around quite a bit.

title: Economy vs Markets
section: S&P 500 sector returns — drill by decade → year → sector
text:
S&P 500 sector returns — drill by decade → year → sector
Treemap with native drill-down. Click a decade tile to zoom into its years; click a year tile to see its sector-by-sector return breakdown. Sector tile size is scaled by absolute return; color shows sign (green = positive, red = negative).

title: Economy vs Markets
text:
Cross-indicator heatmap
Correlations across indicators, 2001–2025. Click a cell for the exact value.

title: US Economy 2026
text: This site is an interactive drill-down analysis of the US economy in 2026 placed against the past 25 years (2001–2025). Use the top navigation to move between the four panels, or click the links below.

title: US Economy 2026
text:
Where to go next

Economic Growth → — GDP growth, quarterly detail, and GDP components (consumption / investment / government / net exports).
Indicators → — Unemployment, inflation, rates, VIX — filterable table with inline sparklines; click a row for the full time series.
Economy vs Markets → — Do markets actually track economic growth? Correlation scatter, dual-axis view, and sector treemap.

title: About
text:
samcaldwell.info is a static, open-source interactive analysis of the US economy in 2026 placed against the prior 25 years. Every chart is an interactive JavaScript widget rendered into a self-contained HTML page.
This prototype ships with a calibrated synthetic dataset — historical values are hand-entered references based on public releases, and values for years ≥ 2025 are illustrative (not forecasts). No proprietary data is redistributed. See citations below.

title: About
text:
Data provenance
Years 2001–2024 — values are hand-entered from public references at the time of prototyping. Because official series are revised periodically, these may differ slightly from current vintage-adjusted values.
Years 2025 and onward — values are illustrative prototype values. They are marked prototype = 1 in the annual CSV and visually flagged with an asterisk (*) throughout the site.
Quarterly and monthly series — decomposed from the annual values with seeded pseudo-random noise such that the within-year averages match the annual figure. High-frequency movements are therefore illustrative, not real historical paths.
Sector returns — derived from synthetic S&P 500 returns via published historical sector betas plus noise. Not a reconstruction of actual sector performance.

title: About
text:
Citations

Public-domain US government data
US government works are in the public domain under 17 U.S.C. § 105 and may be freely reused.

U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. Real Gross Domestic Product [GDPC1]. Retrieved from Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, FRED. https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/GDPC1
U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. National Income and Product Accounts Tables — Gross Domestic Product: Components. https://www.bea.gov/data/gdp/gross-domestic-product
U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. Civilian Unemployment Rate [UNRATE / LNS14000000]. https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/UNRATE
U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics. Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers: All Items in U.S. City Average [CPIAUCSL / CUUR0000SA0]. https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/CPIAUCSL
Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System (US). Federal Funds Effective Rate [FEDFUNDS]. https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/FEDFUNDS
Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System (US). Market Yield on U.S. Treasury Securities at 10-Year Constant Maturity, Quoted on an Investment Basis [DGS10]. https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/DGS10
National Bureau of Economic Research. US Business Cycle Expansions and Contractions (recession dating). https://www.nber.org/research/data/us-business-cycle-expansions-and-contractions



Proprietary third-party data — attribution only, not redistributed
The series below are referenced for analytical context. Their index names are trademarks of the respective owners and are used nominatively; no affiliation or endorsement is implied. This site does not redistribute the underlying proprietary series — historical values are synthesized from public commentary and the site’s current dataset is the calibrated prototype.

S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC. S&P 500 Index®. “S&P®” and “S&P 500®” are registered trademarks of Standard & Poor’s Financial Services LLC, a division of S&P Global. https://www.spglobal.com/spdji/
S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC / Dow Jones & Company, Inc. Dow Jones Industrial Average®. “Dow Jones®” and “DJIA®” are trademarks of S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC and/or Dow Jones & Company, Inc.
Nasdaq, Inc. NASDAQ Composite Index®. “NASDAQ®” is a registered trademark of Nasdaq, Inc. https://www.nasdaq.com/
Cboe Global Markets, Inc. Cboe Volatility Index® (VIX®). “VIX®” and “Cboe®” are registered trademarks of Cboe Global Markets, Inc. https://www.cboe.com/tradable_products/vix/
Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS®) — sector taxonomy is jointly owned by MSCI Inc. and S&P Global. Sector names used here follow GICS Level 1. https://www.msci.com/our-solutions/indexes/gics



FRED terms of use
Where FRED is cited as the retrieval endpoint, the FRED Terms of Use apply: public-domain series are unrestricted; proprietary licensed series may not be redistributed without the data owner’s permission. See https://fred.stlouisfed.org/legal/#copyright.

title: About
text:
Disclaimer
Nothing on this site constitutes investment, tax, legal, or financial advice. The dataset is a calibrated synthetic prototype provided for illustration of the visualization stack; it should not be used as a source of record for any economic, trading, or reporting decision.

title: Economic Growth
text: Use the tabs below to drill from the annual headline into quarterly detail and into the GDP expenditure components. The sunburst at the bottom lets you drill into any single year’s composition interactively (click a wedge to zoom in; click the center to zoom out).

title: Economic Indicators
text:
Rate environment in context
Fed Funds vs 10Y Treasury. When the curve inverts (Fed Funds > 10Y), a recession has historically followed within ~18 months.
